perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees not to use the Confidential I nformation in any fashion, form, or manner for any purpose other than the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that Confidential Information can be used in any fashion, form, or manner for any purpose, including, but not limited to, reverse engineering, except if it directly competes with the other party during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification directly contradicts the core purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which is to restrict the use of confidential information. By allowing broad use, the clause negates the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets, and this modification effectively allows for the misappropriation of trade secrets by allowing the Receiving Party to use the Confidential Information for any purpose, undermining the protection afforded by the DTSA.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intent of a non-disclosure agreement by allowing the use of confidential information for any purpose, except direct competition. This undermines the protection of trade secrets. 18 USC 1836 allows an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action. The modification enables misappropriation, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain the property of the Disclosing Party and no Party acquires any intellectual property rights under this Agreement (including but not limited to patent, copyright and trademark rights).
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain the property of the Disclosing Party; however, the Receiving Party shall have a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license to use, modify, and commercialize such information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates a contradiction by stating the information remains the Disclosing Party's property while simultaneously granting the Receiving Party broad rights to use it, effectively undermining the Disclosing Party's ownership rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act protects the rights of copyright holders. By granting a perpetual license, the modification significantly diminishes the Disclosing Party's exclusive rights as the copyright holder, contradicting the protections provided under copyright law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Chapter 2 - Circular 92 | U.S. Copyright Office Home Copyright Law of the United States (Title 17) Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Copyright Ownership and Transfer PDF Version (94 KB) Back to top 201. Ownership of copyright 1 (a) Initial Ownership.—Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.—In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.—Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106 , may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.—When an individual author’s ownership of a copyright, or of any of the exclusive rights under a copyright, has not previously been transferred voluntarily by that individual author, no action by any governmental body or other offici
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by granting the Receiving Party a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license, which significantly diminishes the Disclosing Party's ownership rights, despite the original text stating that the Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. This directly undermines the Disclosing Party's rights as the copyright holder, contradicting the protections provided under copyright law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically section 201(d)(1), discusses the transfer of ownership of a copyright, but the modified contract language creates a hybrid situation where ownership remains with one party while another party receives a perpetual license, effectively negating key aspects of ownership. This creates a contradiction where the Disclosing Party's ownership is rendered meaningless due to the broad rights granted to the Receiving Party.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of written notice requesting return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will promptly deliver to the Disclosing Party (or, at the Disclosing Party’s option, destroy) all Confident ial Information furnished by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, together with copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of written notice requesting return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party is encouraged, but not obligated, to return or destroy the Confidential Information at their convenience.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the obligation to return confidential information, making it discretionary rather than mandatory. This undermines the Disclosing Party's control over their confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Law (Uniform Trade Secrets Act)
perturbation.law_citation: § 1(2) of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Failing to mandate return or destruction of confidential information after notice significantly increases the risk of misappropriation, thereby weakening the protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon written notice, whereas the changed text makes this action discretionary. This weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially undermining trade secret protection as the Law Explanation states. The change from 'will promptly deliver' to 'is encouraged, but not obligated' directly contradicts the legal intent to protect confidential information, which aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.' The altered language deviates from legally recognized definitions of obligations related to confidential information, which, based on the Law Explanation, creates unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties acknowledge and agree that irreparable harm may occur if any of the Confidential Information wer e to be disclosed to third parties or if any use were to be made of the Confidential Information other than that specified in this Agreement, and the Parties further agree that each shall have the right to seek and obtain injunctive relief upon any violati on or threatened violation of the terms of this Agreement, in addition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge that proving irreparable harm due to disclosure of Confidential Information is inherently difficult, and therefore waive their right to seek injunctive relief, agreeing instead to pursue only monetary damages, capped at $100.
perturbation.explanation: This modification limits the remedies available for breach of the agreement. By waiving injunctive relief, the parties are limited to monetary damages, which may be insufficient to address the actual harm caused by a breach of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law principles, as reflected in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, allow for specific performance or injunctive relief when monetary damages are inadequate. By explicitly waiving injunctive relief, the modification contradicts these standard legal principles, potentially leaving the Disclosing Party without an adequate remedy in case of a breach where the harm is not easily quantifiable in monetary terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of specific performance or injunctive relief, which is a contractual remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2. By waiving the right to seek injunctive relief and limiting the remedy to monetary damages, the modified contract removes a potential remedy available to the parties. The law explanation also mentions this violation, emphasizing that specific performance or injunctive relief is available when monetary damages are inadequate.
